Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Knowing. The Forgiver of sin, the Accepter of repentance, the Severe in punishment, the Owner of abundance. There is no deity except Him; to Him is the destination. (1-3)

All 'Ha-Meem' Surahs Share the Same Character and Temperament

'Ha, Meem' is the Qur'anic name of this Surah. A comprehensive discussion on these abbreviated letters passed at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah. Just remember here that this Surah, and all the subsequent Meccan Surahs included in this group, are designated by this same name and share exactly the same character and temperament.

Favor and Warning to the Opponents

'The revelation of the Book...'—this introductory verse contains within itself both consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and a threat and warning for the deniers of the Qur'an. The aspect of consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) is: 'You have neither composed this Book yourself, nor have you requested it from God to be sent down upon you of your own accord; rather, your Lord Himself has sent it down and is sending it down upon you with highly elaborate care, step by step, so rest assured that He Himself will manifest its truths and He Himself will pave the way for it.' The God Who has sent down this Book is no helpless being, but 'Al-Aziz'—meaning the Dominant and Powerful. He can do whatever He wills, and no one can obstruct His will. But along with being Exalted in Might, He is also Knowing ('Al-Aleem'); for this reason, He knows very well that the conflict initiated by the revelation of this Book is at what stage, how its presenter is being treated, and what the designs of those leading its opposition are, how far they have reached, and when their hand must be seized. The meaning is: this is not the request of a petitioner, but the revealed scripture of guidance from the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God; therefore, both its acceptance and the consequences of its opposition are highly significant and far-reaching.

'The Forgiver of sin, the Accepter of repentance, the Severe in punishment, the Owner of abundance...'—these are additional attributes Allah Almighty has described of Himself: He is the Forgiver of sin and the Accepter of repentance, but also the Severe in punishment and the Owner of great abundance. 'Tawl' means favor, self-sufficiency, power, and bounty. Keeping the principle of contrast in view, I have preferred the meaning of power here.

The Decisive Stage

The meaning is: Allah Almighty possesses both types of attributes and both kinds of authority; therefore, after the revelation of this Book, the way is open for every person to either accept it and become hopeful of God's mercy and forgiveness, or to oppose it and await the manifestation of His punishment and His power.

'There is no deity except Him; to Him is the destination'—meaning if anyone harbors the misconception that there is any other deity besides God, they should correct their misconception. There is no deity besides God. On the Day of Judgment, everyone must return to Him alone, and He alone will decide everyone's affairs; neither will anyone return to anyone else, nor will anyone else be able to intercede for anyone without God's permission.

"No one disputes concerning the signs of Allah except those who disbelieve, so be not deceived by their movement within the land." (4)

'The signs of Allah' refers to the verses of this Book whose revelation was mentioned in verse 2 above. By describing it as 'the signs of Allah' (Ayatillah), its status as a proof and authority is pointed to.

'Those who disbelieve' (alladhina kafaroo) refers here specifically to the Quraysh. It states that the cause of their denial is not that there is any deficiency in the Qur'an's reminding, but rather that they are caught in arrogance and hostility. Their pride makes it highly painful for them to accept that if they deny this Book and its presenter, some punishment will come upon them. Their arrogance prevents them from accepting this, and seeing themselves successful and prosperous in terms of apparent conditions, they mock it; but let no one be deceived by their present prosperity. History is witness that Allah Almighty seized many nations in the very peak of their power and glory, and they could not save themselves from His grasp.

In 'so be not deceived by their movement within the land' (fala yaghrurka taqallubuhum fil-bilad), the address, in terms of apparent words, is to the Prophet (pbuh), but the warning and anger within it is directed at the arrogant of the Quraysh, who in the pride of their authority and leadership were unable to understand that some punishment was about to come upon them. They wondered at the repeated warnings of the Qur'an—asking how and from where the punishment would come upon them!

'Taqallub' means movement, travel, and free coming and going. In this context, it also carries the meaning of pride and arrogance. I have tried to maintain this in the translation.

"The people of Noah denied before them and the companies after them, and every nation plotted against their messenger to seize him, and disputed by falsehood to refute thereby the truth; so I seized them, and how [terrible] was My penalty." (5)

Historical Proof for the Above Promise

This historical proof is presented in support of the preceding statement ('fala yaghrurka taqallubuhum fil-bilad')—showing that before them, the people of Nuh and the nations after them (pointing to 'Aad, Thamud, etc.) similarly denied their respective messengers, and every nation tried to seize its messenger and defeat his truth with their kist-disputes. But before they could lay their hands on the messenger, Allah seized them; so see how severely God seized them as a retribution for their deeds! This is the testimony presented for Allah Almighty's attribute 'Severe in punishment' which was mentioned above, and by using the word 'iqab' (penalty) for the punishment, it is intended to point to the reality that the punishment Allah Almighty sends upon such rebels is the natural retribution of their deeds. It is never any injustice to them. For this reason, one must take warning from their end, but they do not deserve any sympathy.

A Timely Warning to the Quraysh

This verse indicates that this Surah was revealed in that period when the leaders of the Quraysh began to confer to lay their hands on the Prophet (pbuh). The Qur'an warned them that if they intended any such action, they should reflect on its consequences! The consolation within this for the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions (ra) needs no explanation.

"And thus has the decree of your Lord come into effect upon those who disbelieved, that they are the companions of the Fire." (6)

'The decree of your Lord' (Kalimatu Rabbika) refers to that very same decree of punishment which has passed in verse 71 of Surah Az-Zumar. That is: the general decision of Allah Almighty which He uttered in reply to Iblis's challenge—stating: 'Whoever follows you from among the jinn and men, I will fill Hell with you all.' It states: this decision of your Lord, just as it came into effect upon previous nations, has also come into effect upon these disbelievers (the disbelievers of the Quraysh), and they are also going to enter the Fire like them. The meaning is: perform your duty; the end destined for them will be faced by them. Since the punishment of Allah Almighty serves as the introduction to Hell, the punishment is expressed as Hell.

Clarifying the Position of the Angels

Now, it is stated that if these people are relying on the intercession of the angels and their help, and are denying the Prophet's warnings on this hope, they should open their ears and hear that let alone the general angels, even the close angels of God—meaning the bearers of the Throne and those of their category—remain continuously standing in rows, active in His praise and glorification out of fear of God.

'And they believe in Him'—they harbor no claim to divinity, but are believers in Him like God's other servants.

'and ask forgiveness for those who have believed'—and they continuously ask forgiveness for those who believe in God, praying that Allah Almighty protects them from the punishment of both this world and the Hereafter. This asking of forgiveness is their intercession, and it is specified for the believers.

This fear of the angels and their asking of forgiveness for the believers was mentioned in the final verse of the preceding Surah, and has also passed in Surah Ash-Shura in these words:

'And the heavens almost break from above them, and the angels exalt [Allah] with praise of their Lord and ask forgiveness for those on earth.' (Ash-Shura: 5).

The objective of describing this fear of the angels is that those who have assumed regarding them that they are so beloved of God and possess such power over Him that they will never let Him seize their worshippers, should understand that the angels harbor no such claim, but are always seref-gandah before God and seek refuge from His wrath.

The Nature of the Angels' Intercession

'[Saying], "Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy and knowledge, so forgive those who have repented and followed Your way and protect them from the punishment of Hellfire."' This explains the angels' asking of forgiveness—showing that they say no word to their Lord with pride or familiarity, but refer the matter to Allah Almighty's mercy and His knowledge: 'Your mercy encompasses everything and Your knowledge encompasses everything; therefore, You will deal with everyone in accordance with the requirements of Your mercy and knowledge.' With this belief, they pray for forgiveness for those who repent of their lapses and follow the path of Allah. The meaning is: the angels do pray and intercede, but their prayer and intercession is for those who repent of their ignorance and follow the path of Allah, not for those who are the enemies of His messenger and His religion.

The Angels Know that Paradise is Achieved through Repentance and Reform

'"Our Lord, and admit them to gardens of perpetual residence which You have promised them and whoever was righteous among their fathers, their spouses and their offspring. Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise."' This is a further detail of their asking of forgiveness—showing that they pray for Paradise for those who repent and reform to establish their entitlement to it, and for whom Allah Almighty has promised Paradise. They also pray for those among their fathers, spouses, and descendants who establish their entitlement through repentance and reform. The meaning is: the angels are well aware of the reality that no one achieves Paradise merely for the sake of someone else or on the basis of lineage and family, but whoever achieves it achieves it through their own entitlement and the favor of Allah Almighty.

'Indeed, You are the Exalted in Might, the Wise'—this is exactly the same word of intercession that has passed in verse 118 of Surah Al-Ma'idah through the tongue of Hazrat Isa (peace be upon him). It has been explained there. This is a word of leaving the matter to Allah—meaning: You can do whatever You will, but along with this, You are Wise; therefore, You will do only what is based on justice and wisdom. This is the nature of the angels' intercession for the people of the earth, and not what the ignorant have assumed.

'"And protect them from the evil consequences [of their deeds]. And he whom You protect from evil consequences that Day - You have given him mercy. And that is the great attainment."' 'Sayyi'at' (evil consequences) is used here in the sense of the results of evil deeds. To express the relation of the deed with its result, sometimes the verb is used in the sense of the result of the verb—meaning the angels pray for the believers: 'O Lord, protect them from the results of their evil deeds; erase their lapses so that they remain safe from their evil.'

'And he whom You protect from evil consequences that Day - You have given him mercy'—this statement of the angels expresses the terror of the Day of Account: in their eyes, the truly successful is the one whom Allah kept safe on that Day from the results of his lapses. In their view, the greatest success is this, and the truly successful is the one who achieved this success.

The objective of describing this asking of forgiveness of the angels is, as we have pointed out, to clarify that the angels are indeed interceding for the people of the earth, but the nature of their intercession is what has been described, and not that on whose hope people are sitting carelessly, and when they are reminded of the actual reality, they dispute and argue.

The Actual Situation to Be Faced by Those relying on False Intercession

That is, these people are relying on the hope that their intercessors will secure high ranks for them with God, but on that Day, they will be informed through a call: 'Today, the anger and wrath you feel against your own misfortune and the deviation of your leaders is less than the anger and wrath God felt against your state when you were invited to faith through the messenger and you rejected the invitation with great arrogance.' The meaning is: now face the results of this arrogance of yours and His wrath, and beat your heads over your misfortune. Today, no helper is here to protect you. Whatever has appeared before you has appeared after the final establishment of proof from Allah; therefore, you deserve it.

'when you were invited to faith' (idh tud'awna ilal-iman) is the adverbial clause (zarf) of 'the hatred of Allah is greater' (lamaqtullahi akbaru). By 'faith' (iman), faith in Tawhid specifically is meant, because with God, credible faith is only that which is with Tawhid. In verse 45 of Surah Az-Zumar, it has been described that the deniers of the Hereafter feel the greatest aversion to the invitation to Tawhid. Here also, in the subsequent verse, it is clarified that this anger of Allah Almighty upon them will be because when they were invited to Tawhid, they turned away from it, and when partners were associated with Him, they accepted it with great joy: 'That is because, when Allah was called upon alone, you disbelieved; but if partners were associated with Him, you believed. So the judgement is with Allah, the Most High, the Grand.' (12).

Admission of Guilt after the Time has Passed

At that time, these people will say with great humility and clarity: 'O our Lord, now we admit all our crimes. We considered being raised alive again after death impossible; because of this, we remained carefree of Your grasp and reckoning, and mocked Your messenger and his invitation. But now, You have given us death twice and life twice, making us observe the life after death clearly; so is there now any way to get out of this Hell so that we may go to the world anew to lead a life of faith and righteous deeds!' By 'death twice', first is meant that state of non-existence which man experiences before being brought into existence in this world, and second is that death which every living being must face. Similarly, by 'life twice', first is meant the life achieved in this world, and second is that which will be achieved on the Day of Judgment.

This is the reply that will be given to them on behalf of Allah Almighty: 'Whatever has happened to you is the result of your own stubbornness and defiance. When you were invited to Tawhid, you turned away from it, and you remained great supporters of shirk; so now the judgment is with the Most High, the Grand God, and His decision in your favor is this which you are facing.'

Mercy and Punishment Are in the Control of God Alone

That is, both mercy and punishment are in the control of God. For this reason, fear must be of Him alone, and hope must be in Him alone. He continuously makes people observe both of these aspects of His. He makes them observe thunder, lightning, and thunderbolts from the sky, and He also sends down the rain which opens the doors of all provision and favor. This is clear proof that both the sky and the earth are under the control of one God, and in His hand are the thunderbolt of punishment and the treasures of provision and favor.

To Benefit from the Signs, Devotion is Required

'and none remembers except him who turns [to Him]'—meaning these aspects of God are continuously manifest to people for their teaching and reminding, but to benefit from them, it is necessary that the desire to turn to Him and understand is present within a person. If someone becomes so blind in following their desires that they possess no desire to look at anything else and take warning from it, then no sign can open their eyes.

Inviting the Believers to Resolve

This is an address to the Muslims. It states: if these polytheists are not ready to open their eyes, and they are displeased with your invitation to Tawhid and fight with you, then do not care about them; rather, in their opposition, call upon your Lord alone without associating any partner, and worship Him with sincere obedience.

That is, Allah Almighty is of highly elevated ranks, and the Master of the Throne of sovereignty of the entire universe. No one has access to Him. These polytheists whom they have made His partners and close ones are their own self-carved close ones; the exalted court of God is above everyone's reach.

Revelation is the Sole Source of the Knowledge of His Pleasure

'He places the inspiration of His command upon whom He wills of His servants'—'Rooh' (inspiration) refers here to 'revelation' (wahy). Describing revelation as 'Rooh' (spirit/soul) has an obvious reason: just as the body achieves life through the soul, the intellect and heart of man achieve life, warmth, and light through revelation. Lord Masih (peace be upon him) pointed to this reality by stating: 'Man does not live by bread alone, but by every word that comes from God.' 'of His command' (min amrihi) points to the reality that this revelation is among the divine affairs whose complete quality and nature cannot be understood by everyone; its quality is understood only by Him Who sends it down, or by the one who experiences it. This same reality is clarified in another place as: 'And they ask you concerning the soul. Say, "The soul is of the affairs of my Lord. And you have not been given of knowledge except a little."' (Al-Isra': 85). Have a look at what we wrote under that verse.

The objective of bringing this clause here is to show that no Jinn or human has access to the exalted court of God to be aware of His unseen. The sole source of knowing His pleasure is that revelation which He sends down upon whomever He chooses from among His servants for this special task. The meaning is: those means of knowing the unseen which these polytheists have devised according to their own assumption are entirely meaningless. The source of knowing His pleasure is only the revelation which Allah sends down upon His messenger, but these unfortunate people have stood up as the enemies of the Qur'an and its presenter. They assume that if Allah Almighty had to make anyone a messenger, He would choose one of them. They do not know that for this office, Allah Almighty chooses whomever He pleases, and whom He chooses is indeed worthy of it; every claimant is not worthy.

'that he may warn of the Day of Meeting'—'the Day of Meeting' (Yawm-at-Talaq) refers to the Day of Judgment, because on that Day, the presentation of everyone must be before God. The objective of revelation and messengership is to warn people of this very Day. This is the primary issue which needs to be understood. If this is understood, the way is opened to understand other issues; and if this is not understood, no step of man can be taken in the correct direction. For this reason, the primary mission of the honorable Prophets has been the guidance to this destination.

Tahjeer:
Nothing is Hidden from God, nor Can Anyone Escape His Grasp

On that Day, every person's apparent and hidden state will stand entirely revealed before God. No matter of anyone will be hidden from Him, such that the need for any testimony or proof would arise, or anyone could speak a lie, or make some wrong interpretation, or make some false intercession.

'To whom belongs sovereignty this Day?'—meaning at that time, when every person will be entirely revealed and helpless before God, the criminals will be addressed: 'Now speak, to whom belongs sovereignty today? Those whom you made partners of God and assumed that they would save you from Him, where did they go?'

'To Allah, the One, the Prevailing'—since no one will have any answer to this question, He answered it Himself: 'Today, sovereignty belongs to Allah, the One, the Prevailing.' Here, neither can anyone become a helper or protector for anyone, nor can anyone alter any decision of God.

Tafseer: That is, today is the Day of the manifestation of perfect justice. Today, every person will receive the recompense for their deeds. No one will face any injustice or unfairness, and this entire work will occur in the blink of an eye. Let no one remain in the misconception that a long period will be spent in this. Allah Almighty is swift in reckoning.

The Meaning of 'Azifah'

'Azifahn' means that which has drawn near. Here, this word is used for the Resurrection. By using this attribute, it is intended to point to the reality: do not consider the Resurrection far; it is standing right nearby. 'Whoever dies, his resurrection has occurred.' On the Day of Judgment, no one will feel that distance which was between them and the Resurrection in the world; rather, every person will think that they had just slept and woke up. This means that for every person, only those days remain in their resurrection which remain in their life in this world. Just as everyone's death is standing by their side, the Resurrection is also present by their side.

Its second aspect is that when a messenger is sent to a nation, he holds the position of God's court for them. If the nation denies him, they are destroyed. This is the minor resurrection for that nation, which is the introduction to the major resurrection. By using this word ('Azifah'), the objective is to warn the Quraysh: do not consider the Resurrection far; the time of their judgment has arrived. A court has been established for them, and with it, let them await the court of the Hereafter which will make the final decision.

'when the hearts are at the throats, choking. For the wrongdoers there will be no devoted friend and no intercessor [who is] obeyed.' This is a depiction of the terror of the Resurrection—showing that on that Day, the hearts of the criminals will be at their throats, and they will be choked with grief. Neither will any voice come out of their own throats in their defense, nor will they have any friend or intercessor who can open their mouth in their support.

With 'intercessor' (shafee'), the attribute 'who is obeyed' (yuta') is meant to strike at the polytheists' illusion—that their deities are so beloved of God and have such power over Him that He must accept their every word. It states: they will have no intercessor with God whose word will be heard.

"He knows that which deceives the eyes and what the breasts conceal." (19)

An Argument against Intercession

Intercession can be of some use only with one who is not aware of the entire situation himself. Allah Almighty knows every single thing of everyone. He is aware of the treachery of the eyes and the secrets hidden in the breasts; so what intercession of anyone can be of any use before Him!

The same is the case with someone turning truth into falsehood and falsehood into truth through their intercession—it is impossible. Every decision of Allah Almighty will be in accordance with the truth, and no one can alter His decision of truth through their intercession.

'And those they invoke besides Him do not judge with anything'—as for their deities besides Allah, they possess no authority over anything, such that they could do anything in support or opposition of anyone.

'Indeed, Allah is the Hearing, the Seeing'—this presents proof for the preceding statement: the Hearing, the Seeing is Allah Almighty alone; so who else has the right to make a decision? and when He is Hearing and Seeing, how is it possible that someone deceives Him through their intercession to get wrong decisions made? and as for these deities who neither see nor hear, on what basis has it been assumed regarding them that they will also decide anyone's affairs!

An Explicit Threat to the Quraysh

This is an explicit threat to the Quraysh—showing that since they are denying the Prophet's warnings with such audacity, have they never traveled through the land to see what was the end of those before them who were greater than them in power and in terms of their cultural and architectural monuments? When God's punishment seized them for their crimes, neither did their power and numbers avail them, nor could their cultural developments protect them, nor could those deities whom they considered their helpers protect them. This refers to 'Aad, Thamud, and the people of Madyan, etc., whose power, numbers, and cultural developments have been described in detail in the previous Surahs.

The word 'ashad' (greater) here also encompasses the meaning of 'greater and more abundant'; because of this, mentioning 'power' (quwwah) along with 'monuments' (atharan) is entirely fitting. 'Athar' refers to cultural and architectural monuments. In the world, these monuments have always been considered the proof of a nation's greatness. But the Qur'an clarifies that if the nation is devoid of faith, these monuments are the signs of its decline, and ultimately they turn into tombs for its national existence.

This describes the cause of these nations' destruction—showing that their messengers came to them with highly clear proofs, but in the pride of their power, numbers, and worldly developments, they did not care about the messengers' warnings; ultimately, Allah Almighty seized them, and when He seized them, they could not escape from His grasp. Allah Almighty is Powerful and Severe in punishment; when He seizes, no science can save from Him.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 23–55

Next, the story of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and Pharaoh is described, whose primary objective is to present proof for the claim made in the preceding verse: when a messenger is sent to a nation and the nation denies him, they are necessarily destroyed, regardless of their greatness in power, glory, culture, and architecture. By narrating this story, the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions (ra) are consoled—stating: continue with your work with patience, the same fate will be faced by these pharaohs of the Quraysh if they do not change their way. At the same time, within this story, the story of a believing man of the family of Pharaoh is described—who, for a time, kept his faith concealed for some wisdom, but when he saw that an ultimate danger had appeared for Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and his invitation, he came openly to the field and dedicated his life to support Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him).

While the lessons of his story will follow under the commentary of the verses, keep some of its prominent aspects in view here:

* This is narrated to the leaders of the Quraysh to show that the true welfare of their nation is what this believing man did, and not what you are doing according to your own assumption. When this believing man saw that his nation had set out on the path of destruction, putting all his interests aside, he placed himself in danger to stop them; whereas you are dedicating all your power and wealth to drive your nation toward God's punishment.

* Those who kept their faith concealed for some wisdom were taught through this story: keeping the faith concealed is permissible only as long as it is beneficial for the faith and the believers. If an ultimate danger appears for the faith and the believers, then putting all interests aside, every person must come openly to the field like this believing man.

* The weak and oppressed Muslims were taught: those who, like this believing man, play the game of life for the elevation of the word of truth, Allah Almighty is their Helper and Protector.

Recite the verses in this light.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

'signs' (ayat) refers to those commands and instructions which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) brought to Pharaoh and his chiefs, and 'clear authority' (sultan mubeen) refers to the miracle of the staff, which held the position of a divine credentials bestowed upon Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) so that no doubt remained in the minds of Pharaoh and his chiefs regarding his being a divine messenger. The word 'sultan' has occurred in the Qur'an in the sense of credentials, authorization, and authority. Its analysis has passed in its proper place. Further explanation will follow under 'you cannot pass except by authority' in Surah Ar-Rahman.

Mention of Pharaoh and His Two Prominent Leaders

Here, along with Pharaoh, his two prominent leaders are also mentioned who were most active in opposing Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). Both of them have been mentioned in detail in the previous Surahs. Among them, Qarun (Korah) was an Israelite by lineage, but he had accumulated immense wealth under the protection of Pharaoh's government, and this wealth became the primary cause of his opposing Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). His mention has passed in Surah Al-Qasas, and we pointed out there that there is great resemblance between his states and those of Abu Lahab. Here, by mentioning these leaders of Pharaoh, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled—showing that just as the leaders of the Quraysh have stood up in your opposition, the leaders of Pharaoh stood up in opposition to Musa (peace be upon him), but God disgraced them. The same fate will be faced by these leaders of the Quraysh if they do not desist.

'and they said, "A magician and a liar"'—meaning they attributed Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) miracle to magic, and declared his claim that God had sent him as His messenger to be entirely a lie.

The Opposite Effect of Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) Invitation upon the Egyptians

That is, the message of truth which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) brought to them, its right was that they accepted it and reformed their way, but its effect upon them was entirely opposite. Realizing that Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) wanted to make his people a organized and powerful nation, they began to execute with full intensity that policy of killing the male children of the Children of Israel and keeping the female children alive to make them slave-girls, which had been ongoing from long before the birth of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). 'And the plot of the disbelievers is not but in error'—meaning this policy of killing the male children of the Children of Israel, as we have explained, was adopted to keep their numbers in check so that they do not grow to become a threat to the Copts' authority; but all the plans adopted to execute it failed, and their numbers continuously increased, until Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) came to the field with his open challenge, and what the Egyptians feared became a reality before their eyes.

The Panic of Pharaoh and His Chiefs

Due to the failure of their plan and the demand of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), Pharaoh and his chiefs became entirely panicky. In this panic, Pharaoh proposed to his chiefs: 'Let me kill Moses. If there is any god who has sent him as a messenger, let him call upon him for his help.' In support of his proposal, he gave the proof: 'If Moses is given further respite, I fear that he will either change your religion or cause rebellion in the land.' It should be kept in mind that Pharaoh was considered by his people to be the manifestation of the sun-god, and held the status of an avatar king. For this reason, Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) invitation to Tawhid directly struck at his divinity. If the Copts accepted it, their religion would change; and if they did not accept and the Children of Israel became organized under Hazrat Musa's leadership, the logical consequence (in Pharaoh's view) was that rebellion would break out in the land. Because of these dangers, he requested his chiefs' permission to kill Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him). The word 'dharooni' (let me) clearly indicates that Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and his invitation had achieved such strength in that period that even for an absolute ruler like Pharaoh, it was impossible to lay his hand on him without the support of his chiefs.

Hazrat Musa's Attitude

When Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) came to know of Pharaoh's intention, he referred his affair to his Lord—stating: 'Indeed, I have sought refuge in my Lord and your Lord from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account.' 'your Lord' is used here by way of warning—meaning: remember that He is your Lord as well, and there is no other lord besides Him on whose strength anyone can do anything.

'from every arrogant one who does not believe in the Day of Account': Arrogance is, in reality, the name of turning away from the truth, which is the result of pride and self-worship, and this is produced by a lack of faith in the Hereafter. A person who does not believe in the Hereafter is an absolute ruler; there is nothing which he cannot commit.

The Story of a Believing Man

From here begins the story of a believing man. He belonged to Pharaoh's royal family, but was highly upright and God-fearing; because of this, all his sympathies were with Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) from the very beginning. His mention has passed in Surah Al-Qasas. When Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) accidentally killed a Copt and the chiefs of the government began to confer to kill him, he informed Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) of this intention and advised him to leave Egypt, following which Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) went to Madyan. This shows that even before Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) prophethood, he possessed sympathy for his reformative activities.

Following Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) return from Madyan and his declaration of prophethood, he believed in his invitation, but kept his faith concealed for a time. He probably felt that by keeping his faith concealed in that period, he could perform services for Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) and his invitation which he could not perform openly. He was a member of the royal family, enjoying all royal rights. The subsequent verses will show that he was also a member of Pharaoh's parliament. If he had declared his faith at that stage, the Egyptians would have immediately accused him of being a national traitor and turned the entire nation against him.

Observing Discretion in the Matter of Faith is Permissible Only as Long as It is Beneficial

But observing discretion in the matter of faith is permissible only as long as some benefit for the faith is expected from it. If the matter exceeds this stage and an ultimate danger appears for the faith, then keeping the faith concealed becomes hypocrisy. Thus, this believing man kept his faith concealed only as long as he saw benefit in it. But when he saw that Hazrat Musa's (peace be upon him) very life was in danger, he threw off the veil of discretion, stood in the full court of Pharaoh, defended Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), and declared his faith openly.

This event was narrated during this critical stage of the Prophet's (pbuh) invitation to clarify: when the enemies are conferring to kill the Prophet (pbuh), now it is not permissible for anyone to keep their faith concealed; rather, like this believing man, everyone must come to the field dedicating their life.

The Implications of a Phrase of the Believing Man

'"Would you kill a man [merely] because he says, 'My Lord is Allah' while he has brought you clear proofs from your Lord?"'—remember that this believing man said this addressing Pharaoh and all his chiefs. He said: 'O unfortunate ones! Would you kill a man merely because he says: "My Lord is Allah," while he has also brought highly clear proofs from your Lord?'

Within this single phrase, he placed several realities before Pharaoh and his chiefs.

* First, accepting Allah as Lord is such an obvious reality that there is no room for difference in it. If a person says so, he is expressing a highly clear truth, for which he deserves support and appreciation, not death. Those who dare to kill such a person will prove to be highly unjust.

* Second, the proofs he has brought are a clear proof of his being a messenger of God. Only the blind can deny their being divine.

* Third, the Lord Whose messenger he claims to be is your Lord as well. It is your own ignorance that you have taken others besides Him as lords.

A Warning

'"And if he should be a liar, then upon him is his falsehood; but if he should be truthful, there will strike you some of what he promises you"'—this informed them of the consequence of their attempt to kill: 'If he is a liar, as you assume, then the consequence of it will be upon him; and if he is truthful (as is the actual reality), then remember that some of that punishment of which he is warning you will necessarily descend upon you.' This points to that Sunnah of Allah which is appointed for the deniers of the messengers or those who intend to kill them. His meaning was: do not be blinded by emotions to look at only one aspect of the issue; rather, look also at its other aspect, which is highly severe. You have assumed him to be a liar, because of which you consider killing him an easy thing; but what do you know, if he is truthful, then remember that this action of yours will prove to be highly costly. For this reason, safety lies only in leaving him. Do not prepare your own destruction by attempting to kill him.

A Style of the Language

'"And if he should be a liar"' (wa-in yaku kadhiban)—he said these words keeping in view the addressees' thought, which clarified the reality that the doubt expressed in the conditional style is not always for expressing real doubt, but sometimes it is used when there is only some assumption of it. The actual decision depends on the context. This style of the language should be kept in view, as it will assist in understanding other verses.

A Beautiful Satire on Pharaoh

'"Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar."' 'Yahdee' (guide) is used here in the sense of making someone successful in their effort and objective. This is a highly beautiful phrase. Apparently, it is a wise general rule that Allah does not make successful the one who is a transgressor and a liar; but if you reflect, it contains a highly beautiful satire on Pharaoh, which he made right in his face in his full court.

The speech of the believing man became gradually clearer. Addressing the chiefs of the nation, he said: 'O my people, sovereignty is yours today, you being dominant in the land. You can do whatever you please, and no one can hold your hand. But reflect on this question with seriousness: if as a result of this, God's punishment comes upon us, who will protect us from His grasp?'

An Inappropriate Intervention by Pharaoh

'Pharaoh said, "I do not show you except what I see, and I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct."' This speech of the believing man was being delivered in the full court before Pharaoh; because of this, Pharaoh intervened and said to the chiefs: 'The proposal (regarding killing Moses [peace be upon him]) I have placed before you is my well-considered opinion, and with it, I am guiding you to the correct policy.' His meaning was: let no one assume that I have made this proposal in haste under the influence of emotions; rather, I have reflected on its consequences, and this policy alone is correct. If this is not adopted immediately, its results will be highly fatal for this country.

In 'dominant' (zahirina), the state (hal) is from the pronoun 'laku' (is yours).

From this intervention by Pharaoh, it is learned that he estimated: if this believing man's speech continues without intervention, many of his chiefs will be influenced; because of this, like clever political leaders, he tried to establish his sincerity, correctness of opinion, and policy.

The Believing Man Continued His Speech

The believing man continued his speech without caring about Pharaoh's intervention. He said: 'O my people, indeed I warn you that if you attempt to harm Moses (peace be upon him), the punishment will come upon you exactly as it came upon the previous nations—meaning the people of Nuh, 'Aad, Thamud, and those after them. Those nations attempted to harm their messengers, and consequently were destroyed; similarly, you will also be destroyed if you follow in their footsteps.'

'And Allah wants no injustice for [His] servants'—and remember also that Allah Almighty is highly Merciful to His servants. For this reason, before sending any punishment, He sent His messenger to warn you so that those who wish to repent and reform may do so. If, instead of valuing this mercy and favor of Allah Almighty, you attempt to kill His messenger, it means that proof has been fully established against you by Allah, and you have prepared your own destruction.

From this verse, it is learned that Pharaoh came after 'Aad and Thamud, and these nations were adjacent to his country, whose states were so well-known that they could be presented before the people of that era for warning.

The Meaning of 'Yawm-at-Tanad'

'Yawm-at-Tanad' literally means 'the Day of Calling'. This is used to express that Day of punishment of which they are being warned. When some great panic occurs, there is the clamor of running, escaping, holding, and leaving everywhere; because of this, this is a highly suitable word to express the Day of punishment. In this lies a reminder: today, respite has been granted by Allah Almighty, because of which you are making plans to attack Allah and His messenger, but when the attack comes from Allah Almighty: 'The Day you will turn your backs fleeing; there will be for you no protector from Allah'—at that time, you will flee, but this fleeing will be entirely useless, because no protector can save from God's grasp. At that time, you will call out, but this call will be in vain.

'And whoever Allah leaves astray - for him there is no guide'—meaning: 'My job is to warn you of good and evil, and I am doing that. Accepting my advice or not accepting is in your choice. If you commit that action which you are intending, I can say nothing but: those whom Allah leaves astray, no one can guide them.' This refers to that divine law which has been discussed in various places.

The History of the Nation's Deviation and Its Philosophy

He described the history of their deviation—showing: 'Before this, Joseph (peace be upon him) also came to you with clear proofs, but you remained in doubt of that which he brought to you.' The meaning is: although due to the special devotion of the king of that time to Hazrat Yusuf (peace be upon him), no hostile attitude was adopted toward his mission, yet his teachings were not valued. Rather, finding them contrary to the desires of the self, you remained indifferent and in doubt regarding them.

'until when he died, you said, "Never will Allah send a messenger after him"'—meaning, instead of considering him and his teachings as a mercy of Allah, the people considered them a burden. Thus, when he died, people took a breath of relief that this burden was removed, and became assured that now Allah would not send any other messenger who would try to restrict their desires as he did.

'Thus does Allah leave astray him who is a transgressor and doubter'—meaning your present deviation is the progeny of that previous deviation. Those who cross the limits of Allah purely in following the desires of the self, and choose the path of doubt instead of knowledge and certainty in following the self, Allah drives them on that very path of deviation which they chose. Then, they are never guided.

The principle upon which Allah deals with His servants has been pointed out in various places—showing that He desires that people value the gnosis of good and evil and the intellect He has blessed them with. For those who value them, He opens further paths of guidance; and those who do not, but under the influence of the desires of the self try to make even the most obvious truth doubtful, dedicating all their intelligence to this purpose, He does not guide them, and in retribution for this, He strips away even that light of nature which is imbued within every human. Here, this believing man pointed to this reality, and this is highly worthy of attention for those who are dedicating all their energy today to make highly obvious realities doubtful.

The End of Arbitrary Disputing in the Matter of Religion without Proof

That is, whatever discussion is made regarding the verses of Allah and His commands must be based on authority and proof. Those who oppose them without any such proof are highly hated in the sight of Allah and the believers.

'Thus does Allah seal over every heart [of anyone] arrogant and tyrant'—from this clause, first, it is clarified: those who resort to such disputing do so because Allah Almighty seals over their hearts, because of which their intellects are turned upside down and they are not ready to accept any word contrary to their desires, no matter how clear it might be.

Second, it is clarified that this sealing of their hearts is due to their arrogance and their Pharaonism. These people, out of pride, oppose every word contrary to their desires. Even if a prophet warns them, they are not ready to tolerate it—wondering why he became a prophet when they themselves are more worthy of prophethood!... In this era, many self-styled individuals mock highly clear teachings of Islam, while they do not know even the ABC of religion. If anyone reproaches them for this audacity, they reply that no group has a monopoly over religion, and they also have the right to speak on it. In the minds of these people too, this very pride is settled.

An Intervention by Pharaoh

Above, you saw that the believing man's speech gradually reached its peak, which naturally influenced the chiefs of the court. For this reason, Pharaoh intervened again to fool his chiefs, and left an intervention. Addressing Haman, he commanded: 'Haman! Construct for me a high tower so that I might reach the pathways of the heavens and look at Moses's deity whom, according to his assumption, has sent him as a messenger. Indeed, I think he is a liar.'

'Thus was made attractive to Pharaoh...'—meaning even after such a clear speech, Pharaoh was not guided. His concern was to protect his chiefs from the influence of this speech. The lack of guidance is because those who commit evils deliberately become so bold that their evils are made attractive in their eyes, and leaving them becomes painful for them. Thus, Pharaoh also faced this. His bad deeds stood as obstacles in his way and prevented him from choosing the straight path.

'and the plot of Pharaoh was not but in ruin'—meaning Pharaoh said this merely to deceive his chiefs and humiliate Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him); therefore, the Qur'an declared it a 'plot' (kayd) which became a source of destruction for him and his nation.

Pharaoh's Reply

This believing man did not pay any attention to Pharaoh's intervention either. Rather, in highly clear words, he invited the nation: 'O my people, follow me, I will guide you to the way of right conduct.' Obviously, this is a reply to Pharaoh's statement: 'and I do not guide you except to the way of right conduct.' This proved that he warned the nation in highly clear words that in following Pharaoh lies the destruction of the nation. If success is desired, people must follow them.

Reminding of the Hereafter

He challenged Pharaoh, but did not boast of his numbers and power like worldly leaders; rather, in the manner of the honorable Prophets, he reminded people of the Hereafter—showing that this worldly life and all its comfort are temporary, and the permanent home is the Hereafter; therefore, one must not ruin the eternal life for the sake of this temporary comfort.

This describes that rule of reward and punishment which will be established in the Hereafter—showing that those who reach there having committed sins will receive in return only the like of their evil deeds, so that no injustice is done to them, but those who reach there having earned virtues will enter Paradise, wherein they will be given provision without account... It should be kept in mind here that this address of the believing man is to the aristocracy, most of whom desired only the comfort of this temporary life and for whose sake they agreed with Pharaoh. He drew their attention: do not sacrifice the sovereignty of the eternal life for the sake of this temporary comfort.

Replying to the Objections of Some Chiefs of the Nation

The context indicates that just as Pharaoh intervened in his speech, some chiefs of the nation also raised some objections to him at this stage. He did not pay any attention to Pharaoh's intervention, as he had no hope of any good from him. But he replied to the leaders of the nation, and replied with great duls-o-and sympathy. Those leaders had, obviously, raised the objection: 'All your speech is against those deities whom our fathers worshipped. You must remain upon the religion of your fathers instead of establishing a new religion.' This believing man replied to them with great sympathy: 'This is a strange thing: I invite you to salvation, while you invite me to the Fire. I invite you to worship the Exalted in Might, the Forgiving God, Who can seize and is also Forgiving; and you invite me to associate with Him that of which I have no knowledge.' The meaning is: as far as the One Exalted in Might, Forgiving God is concerned, He is accepted by all. Even those who are polytheists believe in Him, and there is no escape from Him. But as for His having partners, this is a thing which stands in need of proof, and I have no proof of it. If I associate any partner without proof, what face will I show to God?

Within 'to disbelieve in Allah and associate with Him' (li-akfura billahi wa ushrika bihi), it is clarified that there is no difference in reality between shirk and disbelief. Whoever associates partners with God is, in reality, committing disbelief, because in religion, merely believing in God is not accepted, but He must be accepted with all His attributes and rights, and among these rights, the greatest is the acceptance of His Tawhid.

'La jarama' means 'la budda' (without doubt). This is a reality; there is no room for dispute or doubt in it. In progress, this also comes in the sense of an oath.

'has no [response to a] call' (laysa lahu da'watun)—the negation of the verb is in the sense of the negation of its benefit. That is: calling upon them is of no benefit in this world, nor will it be of any benefit in the Hereafter. The explanation of this has occurred in other places in the Qur'an.

Summary of the Discussion

Having established the proof, this believing man placed the summary of the discussion before them—showing that this is a certain fact: those idols you are inviting me to worship are merely the creations of your assumption. Calling upon them is of no benefit in this world, nor will it be of any benefit in the Hereafter. In this world, whatever we receive, we receive from God, and in the Hereafter, the return of all must be to God alone. None of these assumed deities will be a protector or place of return.

'And that the transgressors are companions of the Fire'—'transgressors' (musrifeen) refers to those who committed shirk to wrong their own souls. The meaning is: this is also a reality in which there is no room for doubt: those who wrong their own souls will have no intercessor, but all such people will enter the Fire.

The Final Compassionate Warning

This is the final, advisory, and compassionate phrase of this believing man's speech. He said: 'Whether you accept my word today or do not accept, in the subsequent stages, you will remember what I say to you, but at that time, remembering them will be of no use to you.' This points to the reward and punishment of the Hereafter, and also to that punishment of which he warned his nation in case of denying the messenger. Obviously, when the punishment appears or the Hereafter stands before them, they will indeed remember these words and regret, but this regret of theirs will be entirely useless.

'and I entrust my affair to Allah'—meaning: 'I have said what I had to say. Now, do whatever you please. If you stand up as my enemy because of this word of truth, I entrust my affair to Allah. He is the Protector and Guardian of His servants.' 'Indeed, Allah is Seeing of His servants.'

The Failure of Pharaoh's and His Chiefs' Plots

From this, it is learned that after his speech, Pharaoh and his chiefs stood up as his enemies, and began to plot against him to return him to their religion. But Allah Almighty protected him from the evil of their plots. This proves that the plots Pharaoh and his chiefs made to turn him away from the religion of truth achieved no success. Similarly, those servants of Allah who declare the truth, Allah Almighty protects their faith.

The Fate of Pharaoh and His Followers

'and a bad punishment enveloped the people of Pharaoh'—'the people of Pharaoh' (Aal-e-Pharaoh) refers to his family and followers. It states: Allah Almighty protected the believing man from the evil of the Egyptians, but a bad punishment enveloped Pharaoh and his followers. By 'bad punishment', that decisive punishment is meant which drowned Pharaoh and his entire army. The reason for describing it as 'bad punishment' is that during the invitation of Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him), various punishments came upon the Egyptians, but those were to warn and alert, in which both believers and disbelievers were tested; but this final punishment came to protect the believers and completely destroy Pharaoh and his descendants. The word 'haqa' (enveloped) shows that this punishment enveloped them in such a way that no escape remained for them.

The State of Pharaoh and His Followers in Barzakh

This is the detail of that punishment—showing that in the life of Barzakh (the period between death and resurrection), they are made to observe the Fire in the morning and evening, seeing that their permanent home will be this, and when the Day of Judgment comes, the command will be: 'Cast Pharaoh and his followers into the severest punishment.' In the Qur'an, it is repeatedly clarified: after death, states of reward or punishment are experienced by the righteous and the wicked in accordance with their deeds. This serves as the introduction to Paradise or Hell for them. Then, when the Day of Judgment comes, reward and punishment will appear in their actual form. The mention of the punishment of the grave in the Hadiths relates to this very life of Barzakh.

The State of Leaders and Followers in Hell

Above, we pointed out that the story of the believing man was narrated also to encourage those who hesitate to declare the truth out of fear of their leaders. Now, it is shown: those who are not daring to declare the truth in the world out of fear of their leaders, a Day will come when these leaders and their followers will both be in Hell, bickering with one another. The weak and oppressed will say to the arrogant: 'We were your followers in the world, so can you now carry some of this punishment of Hell which has fallen upon us?' The arrogant will reply: 'The time for complaint has passed. Today, both we and you are to remain in it. Allah has decided between His servants with complete justice'... the meaning is: this excuse will be of no use to anyone—that they were under the pressure of others, because of which they remained followers of falsehood. The responsibility of one's own soul lies upon oneself. A person cannot be exonerated by placing this responsibility on others; therefore, every person, no matter how oppressed they might be, should declare the truth relying on Allah, and remain assured that Allah will protect them, and if death comes to them in this path, this death will be the death of martyrdom.

The Foundation of Wicked Leadership is Arrogance

It should be kept in mind here that for these leaders of disbelief and wickedness, the Qur'an has used the word 'istakbaroo' (those who were arrogant)—meaning those who remained in the pride of their greatness. This is a highly meaningful word to express wicked leadership. In this universe, greatness belongs only to Allah; therefore, every leadership which turns people away from the path of Allah is based on arrogance, and its end is Hell—for both the managers and the followers.

The Final Appeal of the People of Hell and Its Failure

When the people of Hell see that neither do their partners and intercessors come to their rescue, nor can their leaders help them, despairing from all sides, they will appeal to the keepers of Hell: 'Pray to your Lord to reduce for us our punishment even by a single day so that we may take a breath.' They will reply: 'Did your messengers not come to you with clear proofs?' They will say: 'Yes, this is true.' They will reply: 'If this is so, then pray yourselves; we cannot pray for people like you.'

'And the supplication of the disbelievers is not but in error'—meaning at that time, every prayer, appeal, and cry of the disbelievers will be entirely in vain. Neither will their assumed deities hear their calls, nor will their leaders be of any use, nor will there be any hearing with Allah Almighty. All doors of hope will be closed upon them.

The Sunnah of Allah regarding the Messengers

This verse relates to verse 45 above. In the middle, five verses occurred to explain that punishment which Pharaoh, his arrogant chiefs, and their followers will face. It states: just as We helped Musa (peace be upon him) and this believing man, We help Our messengers and those who believe in this worldly life as well, and We will also help them on the Day when the witnesses stand.

Our commentators faced great difficulty in interpreting this verse. This is because in it, the promise of Allah's help in this world is made with great clarity. Their difficulty is because they do not have clear the difference between a messenger (Rasool) and a prophet (Nabi). We have been clarifying this difference in various places, so keep it in view. The Sunnah of Allah regarding the messengers is: they hold the position of God's court for the nation to which they are sent. If the nation denies them, it is destroyed—whether through some divine calamity or through the sword of the people of truth, and whether this occurs during the messenger's life or after he has left the world. The life of every messenger from Hazrat Nuh (peace be upon him) to Hazrat Masih (peace be upon him) is witness to this Sunnah, and we have been clarifying it.

'the Day when the witnesses stand' (Yawma yaqoomu-l-ashhad) refers, obviously, to the Day of Judgment, because on that Day, Allah Almighty will make every prophet and messenger testify as to what he taught. Similarly, nations will be questioned as to what they replied. The angels of God will also present the registers of people's deeds. The details of these matters have passed in Surah Al-Ma'idah and some previous Surahs.

This explains 'the Day when the witnesses stand'—showing that on that Day, the testimonies of the divine witnesses will be so clear, so decisive, and so bright that the excuses of those who wronged their own souls will not be of any use. Even those who want to blame their leaders for their deviation will not have their excuses heard. Their leaders will directly reject their statement, saying: 'You were yourselves shamat-zada that you followed us despite knowing that we were upon deviation; so today, both we and you are equal, and both must face the punishment for our deeds.'

'and for them is the curse, and for them is the evil home'—meaning in response to their excuse, there will be the curse of God and His angels, and for their deeds will be an evil home.

Explaining the Promise of Help

This explains that help which was mentioned above—showing that Pharaoh and his followers fell under the punishment of Allah Almighty, and Musa (peace be upon him) and those who believed among the Children of Israel were blessed with the inheritance of the Book of guidance.

Within 'and We made the Children of Israel inherit the Book' (wa awrasna Bani Isra'eela-l-Kitab), the promise of dominance in the land is implied, because the Book is the collection of divine commands and laws. Its requirement is that the nation which receives it also receives dominance in the land. Thus, the Children of Israel enjoyed this dominance as long as they acted upon their Book.

'a guidance and a reminder for those of understanding'—this describes the objective of the Book: Allah Almighty gave it so that it continuously guides people and reminds them of His matters; but this benefit can be achieved only by those who possess intellect. Those who are devoid of intellect cannot benefit from anything. Within this is a satire on those Children of Israel who deprived themselves of the light of this Book due to their deviation and lack of intellect.

Summary of the Discussion

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh) as a summary of the discussion—stating: continue with your invitation; the promise of Allah is true. This points to that promise which was mentioned in verse 51 above—that Allah Almighty will grant victory to His messengers and those who believe in both this world and the Hereafter.

'And ask forgiveness for your sin...'—this describes the method of achieving this patience and steadfastness, which is the condition for the manifestation of this promise: continuously ask forgiveness for your lapses, and exalt your Lord with praise. We have clarified in its proper place that the means of achieving patience is asking forgiveness and prayer. We have also clarified that in such addresses, the Prophet (pbuh) is addressed not personally, but as the representative of the Ummah. Those who are not familiar with the style of this address are surprised at the attribution of the word 'sin' (dhanb) to the Prophet (pbuh), whereas it has a specific context which we have explained.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 56–85

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, which contain instruction of patience to the Prophet (pbuh), along with the glad tidings of victory, and threats of punishment to the leaders of the Quraysh in both this world and the Hereafter. Between these glad tidings and warnings, references are made to those signs of horizons, ourselves, and morals upon which these glad tidings and warnings are based.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

The Primary Cause of the Deniers' Denial

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: do not care about those who dispute regarding the signs of Allah without any proof or authority. 'Signs' (Ayat) refers to those proofs of Tawhid and Resurrection which have passed above in various styles. It states: the cause of their opposition is not that the truth is hidden from them or that they have any proof against it, but they are opposing only because if they accept your word, they will have to accept your superiority, which would tarnish their pride of leadership.

Glad Tidings of the Dominance of Truth

'they will not reach it' (ma hum bi-baligheehi)—meaning in this pride of theirs, they are not going to succeed; the decision of destiny now is: Allah Almighty will elevate this word of truth through your hands, and your opponents will be disgraced in both this world and the Hereafter.

Glad Tidings of Protection from the Opponents' Evil

'So seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Seeing.'—meaning in this pride of theirs, they will try to harm you, but do not care about them; rather, seek refuge in your Lord, and remain assured that the Hearing, the Seeing is He alone. He will protect you from the evil of these arrogant ones.

Recreating Humans is Easier than Creating the Heavens and the Earth

It states: if these people are in the misconception that recreating people after death is impossible, this is their ignorance. The God Who created the heavens and the earth by His power, why would He be unable to recreate humans! Which is the more difficult task, the first or the second? If Allah Almighty can perform such great tasks, He can perform this task which is far smaller than them.

'but most of the people do not know'—meaning this is an obvious reality which everyone should understand, but the majority of people do not understand even this obvious reality.

This clarifies the moral necessity of the Hereafter—showing that if there is no Hereafter, as they assume, it means this world is a place of lawlessness. In the sight of its Creator, the blind of intellect and those possessing intellect are equal, and the righteous and the wicked are equal. This is self-evidently false, as it negates God's attributes of justice, wisdom, mercy, and power—which is equivalent to negating God Himself. For this reason, the coming of the Resurrection is inevitable. There is no doubt in it, but it is surprising that most people do not believe in this obvious reality.

'Blind' ('A'ma) is used here for those who are blind of intellect and heart, and 'seeing' (Baseer) refers to those who possess insight along with sight and reflect upon Allah's signs.

'little is it that you are reminded' (qaleelan ma tatadhakkaroon) clarifies that these realities are not hidden; but what can be done for those who do not utilize their capabilities of hearing and sight.

The word 'istakbaroo' (were arrogant) is used here in the sense of turning away; the preposition 'an' is its indicator.

No Medium is Required to Ask of Allah

Following the reminding of the Hereafter, this is the reminding of Tawhid: your Lord has proclaimed that no medium or means is required to ask of Me; whoever wishes to ask, let him ask of Me, and I will accept his request. The meaning is: when I have placed no guard at My gate, what is the need to make others intercessors and flatter them? This is a refutation of the polytheists' illusion that the means of reaching God is through their assumed partners and intercessors. It states: Allah Almighty has not placed such mediums between Him and His servants, but every servant can establish a direct relation with Him, and Allah Almighty accepts his supplications.

'I will respond to you' (astajib lakum) indicates that Allah Almighty accepts every correct supplication of His servant. If He does not accept some supplication or delays it, it means His wisdom is in not accepting it or in delaying it. The servant must remain pleased with this, because in it lies his own good. Leaving God to hold others' hands is entirely in vain, because acceptance is in the control of Allah alone, and only He knows His wisdom.

'Indeed, those who disdain My worship...': It states: despite this general proclamation of Mine, those who turn away from My worship purely out of pride should remember that they will soon enter Hell in disgrace as a retribution for this pride of theirs.

In verse 56 above, it has passed that those who were opposing the Prophet (pbuh) were doing so not on the basis of any proof or real doubt, but purely because it struck at their pride of leadership. They thought that if they accepted his word, they would become lower and he would become higher. Pointing to them, He stated: those who turn away from the truth purely out of pride will enter Hell in disgrace.

In these verses, after 'Al-Layl' (the night), 'darkness' (mudhliman), and after 'mubsiran' (bright), 'that you may work' (lita'maloo) are omitted due to contextual clarity. Its explanation has passed.

The Cosmic Proofs for Tawhid and the Resurrection

This draws attention to His signs which simultaneously prove both Tawhid and the Resurrection. From the perspective of lordship, they prove the Resurrection, and from the perspective of harmony, Tawhid. It states: Allah is He Who made the night for you to be cool and dark to rest in, and the day bright for you to work in. This is a great favor of Allah upon people, but most people are not grateful for it.

The meaning is: leaving all other signs aside, if they reflect on the creation of night and day, they will know that their Creator is highly Merciful. He made the day for their economic activities, and the night for rest. Whereas, if He had kept only day or only night, no one could have changed it. The requirement of this mercy of His is that people show gratitude to Him, but most people are not grateful.

The requirement of this mercy and lordship of Allah Almighty is also that He brings a Day in which He rewards those who recognized the right of His blessings and remained grateful, and punishes those who benefited from them but showed ungratefulness. If He did not do so, it would mean that the ungrateful and the grateful are equal in His sight, which is self-evidently false and contrary to His justice.

The harmony of night and day in nurturing man is proof that the things that apparently seem opposites in this universe possess a deep connection. This is proof that a supreme Wise Being is using all these opposites. His will is dominant over all, and He alone is the Creator and Owner of everything: 'That is Allah, your Lord, Creator of all things; there is no deity except Him, so how are you turned away?' (62).

A Warning

This is a warning to the Quraysh—stating: just as your intellect has been turned upside down to deny the simple word of the Qur'an, similarly, nations before you continuously denied the signs of Allah. The meaning is: the end faced by them will also be faced by you if you adopt their way.

The Theme of Tawhid and the Resurrection in a New Style

The same theme of Tawhid and the Resurrection which passed above is explained in a new style—showing: Allah is He Who made the earth a stable place and the heaven a canopy for you, and thus constructed that home in which you live. Their connection and mutual compatibility is clear proof that both are under the control of one God. If they had different gods, this home could not be suitable for your living, nor could its existence be possible. Thus, it is proven that Allah alone is He Who made this home for you, and His is the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth.

'And He formed you and perfected your forms'—meaning after constructing an adorned home, He created you in it, then formed you, and blessed you with this special favor: He made your form highly excellent compared to other creations. 'Ihsan' means performing some work with excellence. In the Qur'an, this is expressed in these words: 'We have certainly created man in the best of statures' (Al-Tin: 4). 'The best of statures' refers, obviously, to the excellent arrangement of physical and spiritual capabilities due to which man achieved superiority over other creations.

'And He provided you with good things'—meaning just as He আراستہ a highly excellent home for your living, He provided you with good things.

'That is Allah, your Lord; so blessed is Allah, Lord of the worlds'—meaning indeed He alone is your Lord and the Lord of this world, and He is highly blessed.

That is, the living and the Giver of life is He alone. Those whom you invoke besides Him are, as is stated in another place, 'dead, not alive'. They are devoid of life. They neither hear nor can help anyone. Calling upon them is entirely in vain; so call upon Allah alone, and obey Him with complete sincerity, because He alone is the deserving recipient of praise, Who is the Lord of the worlds.

A Decisive Reply to the Disputers

This is a decisive reply through the tongue of the Prophet (pbuh) to those who were disputing with him in defense of their ancestral deities. He said: 'Tell them: "Indeed, I have been forbidden to worship those deities whom you worship besides Allah, no matter how much you try, I cannot worship them, particularly when highly clear proofs have come to me from my Lord in this regard. I have been instructed to submit myself entirely to the Lord of the worlds—meaning both my worship and my obedience will be for Him alone..."' This declaration of disassociation was made so that if the opponents hoped to make you soft by putting pressure, they would be completely despaired.

In this verse, some parts of the discourse are omitted in accordance with the well-known style of the Arabic language. We have explained two of these omissions in the translation, and their parallels have passed.

Reference to Some Hidden Aspects of Human Creation

The meaning of the verse is: if man reflects on those stages of his creation, they are sufficient to clarify God's power, wisdom, Tawhid, and the Resurrection. It states: He initiated your creation from dust, then ran your progeny from a sperm-drop. This sperm-drop first takes the form of a clinging clot, then gradually develops into a lump of flesh, and then Allah Almighty brings him out as a child from the womb of his mother.

'then to reach your maturity, then to become old'—meaning after creating you, He nurtures you to reach your youth, and then grants you respite until you reach old age. Thus, before both verbs, the theme of nurturing and granting respite is omitted.

'and among you is he who is taken [in death] before, and that you may reach a specified term'—meaning it does not happen that everyone reaches youth; rather, many die in childhood, and many receive respite only for a specified term. No one receives unlimited life. In this clause, before 'that you may reach' (li-tablughoo), the theme of receiving respite is omitted.

'and that perhaps you will use reason'—this is described as the objective of making human life pass through these stages: He did so so that you reflect on all these stages of your creation, understand His power and wisdom, and believe. We have pointed out that Allah Almighty has made this world in such a way that it serves as an excellent place of learning for those realities whose acceptance is necessary for human success. Here too, before 'that perhaps' (la'allakum), the theme of reflecting on this creation is omitted.

The God Who brings man into existence with such power and wisdom, Who has absolute control over everyone's life, death, youth, and old age, and Who has appointed a specified term for everyone, can He not recreate humans, or can anyone obstruct His will? Thus, it is proven that He is One and has no partner, and He will necessarily gather everyone for reward and punishment, and this task is not in the least difficult for Him. To understand this, have a look at verse 11 of Surah Fatir.

That is, life and death are entirely in His control, and His power is such that when He decides a matter, no one can obstruct His will. He says: 'Be,' and it is in accordance with His command. The meaning is: understand the Resurrection in this way. When He wills to bring it, it will come in the blink of an eye through His single word 'Kun' (Be).

A Threat to the Opponents

That is, these realities are entirely clear, but look at these unfortunate people: how has their intellect been turned upside down that they are disputing to deny the clear signs of Allah.

'The Book' refers to the Qur'an. This describes their crime—showing that they denied the Qur'an and also all those teachings with which We sent Our messengers. The teaching of all messengers has been fundamentally the same as what the Qur'an is presenting; therefore, denying the Qur'an is denying all of them. 'They will soon know' is a threat—meaning if they have committed this audacity, they will soon see its consequence.

After 'and the chains' (was-salasilu), the words 'on their feet' are omitted. We have opened them in the translation. The meaning is: because of their pride, they denied the signs of Allah; therefore, shackles will be placed on their necks and chains on their feet. After this, they will be dragged into boiling water, and then cast into the Fire. 'Sajartul-tannoor' means to fill the furnace with fuel.

The Panic of the Polytheists

After this, they will be asked: 'Now speak, where are those partners whom you set up besides Allah to save you from His grasp? If they exist, call them to save you!' 'Min duni-llah' (besides Allah) is in the sense of 'madd-e-muqabil' (against Allah), and it has passed. Its explanation has passed.

They will reply: 'They have been lost from us; rather, before this we did not call upon anything!' That is, first they will admit that they had deities whom they worshipped, but none is present to help them; but when they feel that they are facing this punishment specifically for their worship, they will deny it—hoping that this denial might be of some use. Parallels in the Qur'an show that on that Day, such panic will overcome the polytheists that they will admit and deny their deities in a single breath. Under verses 23-24 of Surah Al-An'am, this has been discussed.

'Thus does Allah leave astray the disbelievers' points to this very admission and denial of theirs—showing that the punishment of Allah will so panic them that they will have no awareness of what they said and what they are saying. Its explanation has passed above, and this theme is also coming in verse 48 of Surah Fussilat, where further explanation will follow.

Greatness belongs Only to God

That is, whatever has appeared before you is the result of your exulting and boasting in the earth without any right. Whatever you received in the world was not yours, but was the gift of Allah Almighty; therefore, its right was that you became grateful and obedient to your Lord, but you assumed His gifts as your right and fell into pride to boast, and in this pride, you insulted and denied His messengers who wanted to remind you of the actual reality.

Everything in the heavens and the earth is created by Allah Almighty and is His property; therefore, greatness belongs to Him alone, and is not suitable for anyone else. If anyone else shows pride, it is 'without right' (bighayril-haqq), and he is attempting to put on Allah Almighty's specific mantle of greatness, which is shirk. In 'Greatness is My mantle' (Al-Kibriya'u rida'ee), this reality has been reminded.

The Fate of the Arrogant

That is, their excuse and apology is entirely useless. They will be told: 'These are the gates of Hell opened for you'—pointing to the seven gates of Hell whose details have passed. It will be said: 'Enter them, to abide eternally therein.' This will be said turning away from them—implying: wretched indeed is the residence which will be faced by these arrogant ones!

Consolation for the Prophet

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: observe patience over their activities. The punishment of which they are being warned, if they do not change their way, will necessarily stand manifest. Either some part of it will be shown to you in your life, or they will return to Us, and We will punish them in the Hereafter. It should be kept in mind here that the messengers of Allah Almighty warned their nations of two kinds of punishments: one which is faced in this world if the nation denies the messenger, and the second which is faced in the Hereafter.
